question: why does he start running all of a sudden?
ground_truth_answer: He suddenly realizes he might lose access to his own time and be left helpless in a strange world. That fear hits him so strongly that he panics and runs back as fast as he can to where he left the machine.
context_hint: came the possibility of losing my own age

hint: came the possibility of losing my own age

question: difference between moving in time and moving in space here?
ground_truth_answer: Moving in time would mean using the machine itself to travel to another age. Moving in space means it was physically taken and hidden somewhere nearby in the same time. He concludes it was moved only in space because the levers had been removed, which prevented anyone from operating it in time.
context_hint: It had moved, and was hid, only in space

hint: It had moved, and was hid, only in space

question: What is the sphinx doing in this scene, basically?
ground_truth_answer: The sphinx is not doing anything physically, but it dominates the scene as a looming, eerie presence. Standing above him in the moonlight, it seems to smile mockingly at his distress, which adds to the feeling of helplessness and mystery.
context_hint: It seemed to smile in mockery of my dismay

hint: It seemed to smile in mockery of my dismay

question: walk me through what he does after he finds the machine missing, because i got a little lost
ground_truth_answer: After he reaches the lawn and sees the machine is gone, he feels faint and cold. He runs around the area wildly, as if it might be hidden nearby, then stops in shock beneath the sphinx. After that he reasons that the machine could not have been moved in time, only hidden in space. Then he falls into a frenzy, searching among the bushes, startling a white animal, and even beating the bushes until his knuckles bleed. Finally, sobbing and nearly out of his mind, he goes down to the large stone building, where the hall is dark and empty, and he lights a match to keep going.
context_hint: When I reached the lawn my worst fears were realised

hint: When I reached the lawn my worst fears were realised

question: what were they eating?
ground_truth_answer: They were eating fruit, and fruit was all their diet.
context_hint: Fruit, by the bye, was all their diet.

hint: Fruit, by the bye, was all their diet.

question: give me the quick picture of what the hall looked like
ground_truth_answer: The hall looked rich and picturesque overall, but also worn down and neglected. Its stained-glass windows were broken in many places, the curtains were thick with dust, and even a marble table corner was fractured.
context_hint: the thing that struck me most was its dilapidated look

hint: the thing that struck me most was its dilapidated look

question: difference between the people’s appearance and the condition of the room?
ground_truth_answer: The people appeared uniformly dressed in soft, strong, silky material and seemed orderly in that sense, while the room itself showed decay. The hall had broken stained glass, dusty curtains, and damaged marble, so there was a contrast between rich appearance and physical dilapidation.
context_hint: All were clad in the same soft, and yet strong, silky material.

hint: All were clad in the same soft, and yet strong, silky material.

question: If I’m trying to picture the meal setup, how were they actually dining? Tables and chairs, or something else?
ground_truth_answer: They were seated on cushions scattered between the tables rather than on chairs. They ate fruit with their hands and threw peels and stalks into round openings in the sides of the tables.
context_hint: Between the tables was scattered a great number of cushions.

hint: Between the tables was scattered a great number of cushions.

question: why is he talking about extinct animals here? im trying to connect that to the diet part
ground_truth_answer: It explains how completely fruit-based life had become in that future world. He learned later that horses, cattle, sheep, and dogs had become extinct, which fits with a society that was strictly vegetarian and in practice frugivorous.
context_hint: horses, cattle, sheep, dogs, had followed the Ichthyosaurus into extinction

hint: horses, cattle, sheep, dogs, had followed the Ichthyosaurus into extinction

question: why didnt he just keep using matches the whole time instead of stumbling through the dark?
ground_truth_answer: He didn’t light any matches at first because he had no hand free. He was carrying Weena on his left arm and holding an iron bar in his right hand.
context_hint: I lit none of my matches because I had no hand free.

hint: I lit none of my matches because I had no hand free.

question: What changed his situation from uneasy to openly dangerous?
ground_truth_answer: At first he only heard the forest sounds and a faint pattering behind him, but the danger became immediate when the Morlocks closed in physically. He felt a tug at his coat and something at his arm, which showed they had reached him in the dark.
context_hint: there were evidently several of the Morlocks, and they were closing in upon me

question: camphor?
ground_truth_answer: He uses a lump of camphor as a quick source of flame and light. After striking a match, he prepares to light the camphor so the fire will continue when the match burns down.
context_hint: I hastily took a lump of camphor from my pocket

hint: I hastily took a lump of camphor from my pocket

question: difference between the first fire and the camphor flame?
ground_truth_answer: The first fire was the blaze from his heap of sticks, which spread to nearby bushes and along the grass of the hill. The camphor flame was a smaller fire he deliberately lit and threw to the ground to drive back the Morlocks and the shadows at close range.
context_hint: from my heap of sticks the blaze had spread to some bushes adjacent

hint: from my heap of sticks the blaze had spread to some bushes adjacent

question: wait, was weena fighting him because she was scared of the woods or because she wanted the fire?
ground_truth_answer: She wanted to run to the fire and play with it. He even believes she would have thrown herself into it if he had not restrained her.
context_hint: She wanted to run to it and play with it.

hint: She wanted to run to it and play with it.

question: why does selective breeding improve plants and animals so slowly?
ground_truth_answer: It improves them gradually because human goals are still uncertain and incomplete, human knowledge is limited, and nature does not easily yield to clumsy handling. The idea is that people can make changes, but only in a slow, partial way when they do not fully understand what they are trying to control.
context_hint: We improve them gradually, because our ideals are vague and tentative

hint: We improve them gradually, because our ideals are vague and tentative

question: weight of the argument for 'social paradise'?
ground_truth_answer: The impression comes from visible conditions: people seem well housed, beautifully clothed, and free from labor. There are also no signs of social or economic struggle, and the usual machinery of commerce—shops, advertisements, and traffic—has disappeared, so it looks like a world where major human conflicts have been resolved.
context_hint: There were no signs of struggle, neither social nor economical struggle

hint: There were no signs of struggle, neither social nor economical struggle

question: Can you walk me through why the observer concludes disease had basically been eliminated instead of just reduced?
ground_truth_answer: The conclusion rests on multiple signs pointing in the same direction. The environment itself appears controlled: the air has no gnats, the ground has no weeds or fungi, and the world is full of healthy fruit and flowers. On top of that, there is no evidence of contagious illness during the observer's stay. Taken together, these details suggest not just partial progress but a world where preventive medicine has succeeded so completely that disease has been stamped out.
context_hint: The ideal of preventive medicine was attained. Diseases had been stamped out.

hint: The ideal of preventive medicine was attained. Diseases had been stamped out.

question: seedless grape, sweeter flower — what are those examples trying to show?
ground_truth_answer: They show how people improve living things in small, specific ways through selective breeding. The examples emphasize that human improvement has been limited to a few favored plants and animals, with gradual changes like better fruit, larger flowers, or more useful cattle rather than broad control over nature.
context_hint: now a seedless grape, now a sweeter and larger flower

hint: now a seedless grape, now a sweeter and larger flower

question: difference between the world of 'today' and the later world here?
ground_truth_answer: In the earlier world, sanitation and agriculture are still described as rudimentary, with only partial control over disease and only scattered control over plants and animals. In the later world, nature has been deliberately reordered for human purposes: diseases are gone, pests and weeds are absent, and social life appears free from labor, commerce, and open struggle.
context_hint: the sanitation and the agriculture of today are still in the rudimentary stage

hint: the sanitation and the agriculture of today are still in the rudimentary stage

question: im a little confused about the population part — why would seeing no struggle make someone guess population stopped increasing?
ground_truth_answer: The reasoning is that overcrowding and competition usually create visible pressure—economic conflict, social strain, and struggle over resources. Since none of that appears to be present, the observer infers that one major cause of such pressure, increasing population, must have been brought under control and likely stopped.
context_hint: The difficulty of increasing population had been met, I guessed

hint: The difficulty of increasing population had been met, I guessed

question: why does Weena freak out when he goes to the well?
ground_truth_answer: She becomes upset as soon as she sees him lean over the mouth of the well and prepare to descend. She first watches in amazement, then cries out piteously, runs to him, and tries to pull him back with her hands.
context_hint: when she saw me lean over the mouth and look downward

question: How dangerous was the climb, really? It sounds dramatic, but what actually went wrong down there?
ground_truth_answer: The descent was genuinely dangerous. The shaft was perhaps two hundred yards deep, the metallic bars were made for someone much smaller and lighter, and he quickly became cramped and exhausted. One bar bent suddenly under his weight and nearly threw him into the darkness below, leaving him hanging by one hand for a moment. After that, he felt unable to stop and rest, even though his arms and back were in acute pain.
context_hint: One of the bars bent suddenly under my weight

hint: One of the bars bent suddenly under my weight

question: unstable hooks?
ground_truth_answer: They are the climbing supports around the well that he has to feel for and cling to as he begins going down. He immediately treats them as unreliable, which fits with the later moment when one of the bars bends under his weight.
context_hint: I had to look down at the unstable hooks

hint: I had to look down at the unstable hooks

question: If he was already thinking about turning back, why didn’t he just climb up again instead of forcing it? That seems like a terrible decision.
ground_truth_answer: He does consider going back and leaving the Underworld alone. But even while thinking that over, he keeps descending, and soon reaches a narrow horizontal tunnel where he can finally rest. The account shows hesitation and fear, but also that he continues downward despite them.
context_hint: I had some thought of trying to go up the shaft again

hint: I had some thought of trying to go up the shaft again

question: what was the only light source while he was climbing?
ground_truth_answer: Almost everything was completely dark except the opening above him, which looked like a small blue disc. A star was visible in it, and Weena’s head briefly appeared there as a dark shape.
context_hint: I saw the aperture, a small blue disc

hint: I saw the aperture, a small blue disc

question: Can you walk me through why the descent becomes harder than he expected? I’m not just asking that he got tired — what specific conditions made it fail-prone?
ground_truth_answer: Several things make the descent increasingly difficult. The shaft is very deep, perhaps two hundred yards, so the effort is prolonged. The metallic bars are suited to a creature much smaller and lighter than he is, so his body does not fit them well and he becomes cramped and fatigued quickly. The risk is not only exhaustion: one bar bends under his weight and nearly sends him falling, which leaves him afraid to pause again. On top of that, the darkness strains his eyes, the constant thudding and humming of machinery below grows louder and more oppressive, and the terror of falling leaves him trembling by the time he reaches the side tunnel.
context_hint: these being adapted to the needs of a creature much smaller and lighter than myself

hint: these being adapted to the needs of a creature much smaller and lighter than myself

question: why didnt they believe the Time Traveller?
ground_truth_answer: They distrusted him because he seemed too clever and too subtle. Even when he appeared open and straightforward, they felt there was always some hidden ingenuity or trick behind what he did.
context_hint: The fact is, the Time Traveller was one of those men who are too clever to be believed

hint: The fact is, the Time Traveller was one of those men who are too clever to be believed

question: too clever to be believed meaning?
ground_truth_answer: It means someone seems so intelligent and skillful that other people become suspicious instead of trusting him. His cleverness makes them think he may be hiding something.
context_hint: too clever to be believed

hint: too clever to be believed

question: What made the group less skeptical of Filby than of the Time Traveller?
ground_truth_answer: They felt Filby was easier to understand and his motives would have seemed plain to them. The Time Traveller, by contrast, had a whimsical side and seemed harder to read, so people were more likely to suspect him.
context_hint: Had Filby shown the model and explained the matter in the Time Traveller’s words

hint: Had Filby shown the model and explained the matter in the Time Traveller’s words

question: pork-butcher could understand Filby?? what does that mean
ground_truth_answer: It means Filby seemed like a straightforward, ordinary sort of person whom anyone could understand. The phrase suggests he felt familiar and easy to judge, unlike the Time Traveller, who seemed complicated and elusive.
context_hint: a pork-butcher could understand Filby

hint: a pork-butcher could understand Filby

question: explain what they were thinking about during the week after hearing about time travelling
ground_truth_answer: They did not talk much about time travelling between one Thursday and the next, but the idea stayed in their minds. They thought about whether it might be plausible even though it seemed practically unbelievable, and they also imagined strange results like anachronisms and total confusion. The narrator himself was especially occupied by the trick of the model.
context_hint: its plausibility, that is, its practical incredibleness, the curious possibilities of anachronism

hint: its plausibility, that is, its practical incredibleness, the curious possibilities of anachronism

question: what happened when the narrator came back the next Thursday?
ground_truth_answer: When he arrived late at Richmond, he found four or five men already in the drawing-room. The Time Traveller was absent, and the Medical Man explained that their host had been unavoidably delayed and had left a note asking them to begin dinner at seven if he was not back.
context_hint: He’s unavoidably detained. He asks me in this note to lead off with dinner at seven

hint: He’s unavoidably detained. He asks me in this note to lead off with dinner at seven

question: who was worried about the candle thing, and why?
ground_truth_answer: The Medical Man brought attention to the blowing-out of the candle. He said he had seen something similar at Tübingen and thought that detail was important, even though he still could not explain how the trick worked.
context_hint: laid considerable stress on the blowing-out of the candle

hint: laid considerable stress on the blowing-out of the candle

question: "It is a mistake to do things too easily" — what is that getting at?
ground_truth_answer: It means that when someone does remarkable things too smoothly, people may not admire them and may instead suspect trickery. In the Time Traveller’s case, his ease made impressive things seem less trustworthy rather than more convincing.
context_hint: It is a mistake to do things too easily

hint: It is a mistake to do things too easily

question: why did he wake up so uneasy right before dawn?
ground_truth_answer: He had been sleeping restlessly and dreaming that he was drowned while sea anemones felt over his face. When he woke, he also had the strange impression that some greyish animal had just rushed out of the chamber, which left him uncomfortable and unable to settle back to sleep.
context_hint: dreaming most disagreeably that I was drowned

ground_truth_answer: He went out to the flagstones in front of the palace because he decided to watch the sunrise.

question: walk me through what he actually saw on the hill, because i'm not sure what was real and what he only thought he saw
ground_truth_answer: In the dim half-light before sunrise, he thought he saw white figures on the slope three different times. Twice, he believed he saw a solitary white, ape-like creature running quickly up the hill, and once near the ruins he saw a group of them carrying some dark body. They moved hastily and seemed to disappear among the bushes, but as daylight strengthened and he looked again carefully, he found no sign of them and concluded they may have been only appearances created by the half-light.
context_hint: Three several times, as I scanned the slope, I saw white figures

hint: Three several times, as I scanned the slope, I saw white figures

question: difference between the grey animal in the room and the white figures outside?
ground_truth_answer: The grey animal was only an odd impression he had when he woke, as if something had just rushed out of the chamber. The white figures were shapes he thought he saw outside on the hillside in the uncertain light before dawn.
context_hint: with an odd fancy that some greyish animal had just rushed out of the chamber

hint: with an odd fancy that some greyish animal had just rushed out of the chamber

question: I’m trying to pin down why the sighting sticks with him for hours. If he pretty much decides it was bad light, why doesn’t he just drop it?
ground_truth_answer: He does not fully trust the explanation, because the experience leaves him unsettled. He keeps thinking about the figures all morning, links them vaguely to the white animal he had startled earlier while searching for the Time Machine, and feels that the joke about ghosts does not really satisfy him. Even before anything else happens, the sight has already lodged in his mind as something ominous.
context_hint: But the jest was unsatisfying, and I was thinking of these figures all the morning

hint: But the jest was unsatisfying, and I was thinking of these figures all the morning

question: what changed as evening turned into night?
ground_truth_answer: The distant blue faded, stars began appearing one by one, the ground grew dim, and the trees turned black. At the same time, Weena became more fearful and more tired.
context_hint: one star after another came out

hint: one star after another came out
